core.action: Round each share quantities to integer multiples of 100 (one lot) before order submission; reject any calculated position size not divisible by 100 with clear error message; for stocks where calculated position < 100 shares, either skip the position or allocate minimum lot

consequence.description: A-share market requires trading in integer multiples of 100 shares; submitting orders with non-compliant share counts are rejected by exchanges, causing failed trades and missed entries in live trading while backtests assume successful execution

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-056

core.when: When implementing multi-agent trading systems or portfolio-level decision making
core.action: Assume the framework provides multi-agent decision consensus or risk veto mechanisms — the framework (AKShare) is a data library only; it does not implement multi-agent coordination, voting, or risk veto logic

consequence.description: Without multi-agent consensus, independent agents may execute conflicting trades; in live trading, this causes margin calls, position conflicts, and regulatory compliance violations when multiple strategies manage the same account

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-031

core.when: When designing multi-agent trading architectures using this framework
core.action: Implement an external multi-agent orchestration layer with decision consensus and risk veto rights — define agent roles, voting thresholds, and risk veto conditions in configuration; verify each agent decisions pass through a central risk controller before order submission

consequence.description: Missing multi-agent consensus leads to conflicting orders from different agents; in live trading, this causes position overselling, margin violations, and potential regulatory breaches when automated systems make inconsistent decisions

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-031

core.when: When implementing position management logic for A-share backtesting
core.action: Verify stocks bought today cannot be sold on the same day — control available quantity via trading_t attribute to enforce T+1 settlement rule

consequence.description: Violating the T+1 rule causes the backtest to include same-day round-trip trades prohibited in A-share markets, making backtest results completely non-reproducible in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-030

core.when: When calculating interest accrual, bond pricing, duration, or any time-weighted financial metric in the backtesting framework
core.action: Assume the framework implements day count convention calculations automatically — the framework does not implement day count convention, and no default convention is applied

consequence.description: Without explicit day count convention, accrued interest and bond pricing calculations may use inconsistent or incorrect conventions, causing systematic valuation errors that accumulate over time

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-007

core.when: When implementing any fixed-income, interest, or time-weighted calculation in the backtesting framework
core.action: Explicitly specify and document the day count convention (ACT/365, ACT/360, 30/360, 30E/360) for each time calculations involving dates, and validate that the convention matches the market standard for the instrument being modeled

consequence.description: Missing day count convention leads to inconsistent accrued interest calculations, bond pricing errors, and potentially wrong strategy signals based on incorrectly valued positions

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-007

core.when: When calculating economic indicator growth rates (YoY/MoM) for trading signal generation
core.action: Use YoY formula (current - same_period_last_year) / same_period_last_year for trend analysis (removes seasonality, more stable) and MoM formula (current - previous_month) / previous_month for turning point detection (faster but noisier) — verify period alignment is correct: YoY requires same calendar period last year, MoM requires consecutive periods

consequence.description: Incorrect period alignment in YoY calculations causes seasonal mismatches, producing spurious growth signals and false economic cycle transitions that lead to mistimed trading entries and exits

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-078

validation_threshold: YoY calculation missing same_period_last_year alignment OR MoM calculation missing consecutive period alignment → FAIL

core.when: When implementing Yang-Zhang realized variance combining overnight, Rogers-Satchell, and open-close variance components
core.action: Validate that overnight variance uses correct n-1 denominator and weighting coefficient k=0.34/(1.34+(n+1)/(n-1)) is computed accurately — for small samples (n < 30), the n-1 denominator creates significant weighting coefficient bias that amplifies through the composite estimate

consequence.description: A 5% bias in overnight variance contributes 0.05*(1-k)*Vrs to final RV through the Yang-Zhang composite, multiplying downstream through position sizing and risk calculations and causing systematic over/under-allocation

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-090

validation_threshold: n < 30 AND k calculated with n-1 in denominator → FAIL

core.when: When calculating fashion index returns using percentage change formula (current - previous) / previous
core.action: Verify pct_change() usage: percentage change is additive across assets but NOT across time — for multi-period aggregation, use log returns instead of percentage change to avoid compounding errors

consequence.description: Using simple percentage change for time-additive returns produces compounding errors that accumulate over multiple periods, causing systematic overestimation of cumulative returns and incorrect strategy performance comparisons

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-083

validation_threshold: aggregation_method == 'percentage_change' AND periods > 1 AND expected_log_return_behavior → WARN

core.when: When retrieving futures market data and distinguishing symbol types for analysis
core.action: Correctly distinguish between variety (continuous series), specific contract (expiring), and main contract data retrieval — verify symbol type parameter matches intended data use case, as wrong symbol type returns incorrect price series leading to data contamination

consequence.description: Using variety symbol retrieves continuous series instead of specific contract data, causing price mismatches between backtest and live trading where expiring contract prices differ from continuous approximations

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-016

core.when: When calculating strategy profitability metrics for viability assessment
core.action: Calculate profit factor as gross_profit divided by gross_loss (not net profit ratio or win/loss ratio) — strategies with PF < 1 are unprofitable regardless of win rate, and using wrong formula produces different strategy rankings

consequence.description: Using net profit instead of gross profit/loss ratio misclassifies strategies with high gross profits but low net due to costs as unprofitable, leading to incorrect elimination of viable strategies and missed trading opportunities

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-070

core.when: When implementing portfolio rebalancing logic
core.action: Assume the framework provides an automatic rebalancing trigger mechanism — the framework does not implement rebalancing triggers; portfolio drift will accumulate unchecked without explicit intervention

consequence.description: Without a rebalancing trigger, portfolio allocations drift from targets over time, causing unintended risk concentration and deviation from the investment mandate

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-018

core.when: When implementing portfolio management and rebalancing logic
core.action: Implement a rebalancing trigger that monitors drift_percentage from target allocation; trigger rebalancing when drift exceeds a configurable threshold (e.g., drift > 0.05 for 5% deviation)

consequence.description: Skipping rebalancing trigger implementation allows allocations to drift indefinitely, leading to unintended risk exposure and potential regulatory violations

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-018

core.when: When implementing volatility modeling or risk calculation
core.action: Assume the framework provides volatility model family selection and distribution choice — the framework does not implement volatility model parameterization; GARCH, stochastic volatility, or fat-tailed distributions are not available by default

consequence.description: Without explicit volatility model selection, strategies relying on variance estimates will use naive assumptions, leading to mispriced options and underestimated risk

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-019

core.when: When implementing volatility modeling for risk or pricing calculations
core.action: Explicitly select and configure volatility model (e.g., GARCH(1,1), Heston, EWMA) and distribution (e.g., normal, t-distribution, skewed normal) with validated parameters for the asset class

consequence.description: Using default Gaussian assumptions on fat-tailed return distributions underestimates tail risk, causing VaR and expected shortfall calculations to be dangerously optimistic

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-019

core.when: When computing factor IC (Information Coefficient) for factor analysis
core.action: Assume the framework automatically handles IC demean and group alignment — the framework does not implement cross-sectional IC calculation with proper alignment; raw factor values may be used without normalization

consequence.description: Without IC demean and group alignment, cross-sectional factor rankings are distorted by outliers, producing misleading IC values that misrepresent factor predictive power

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-020

core.when: When computing factor IC for factor research and selection
core.action: Implement IC calculation with cross-sectional demean (subtract cross-sectional mean) and group alignment (align factor deciles with return distribution), validated by checking IC_sign matches expected_direction

consequence.description: Skipping IC demean causes factors with positive mean shifts to appear artificially predictive, leading to incorrect factor selection and wasted research effort

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-020

core.when: When processing PCAF data quality assessments
core.action: Assume the framework provides PCAF data quality scoring — the framework does not implement PCAF-compliant data quality scoring; data quality issues will pass undetected into calculations

consequence.description: Without PCAF data quality scoring, corrupted or incomplete data sources are processed without flags, propagating errors into risk calculations and regulatory reports

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-023

core.when: When ingesting data from PCAF-regulated data sources
core.action: Implement PCAF data quality scoring with dimensions: completeness (>95%), timeliness (<T+1 lag), accuracy (vs. reference source), consistency (cross-source alignment); flag records below threshold as invalid

consequence.description: Missing PCAF scoring causes regulatory non-compliance, as data governance requirements mandate quality metrics for all inputs used in risk calculations

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-023

core.when: When implementing feature extraction for machine learning models
core.action: Assume the framework automatically handles feature extraction time boundaries — the framework does not implement look-ahead protection or boundary handling; features may inadvertently include future information

consequence.description: Without feature extraction time boundaries, ML models are trained on features that include future data, creating look-ahead bias that makes backtest results unreproducible in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-025

core.when: When implementing feature extraction for ML-based trading strategies
core.action: Enforce feature extraction boundaries by ensuring timestamp(feature) <= timestamp(label) - min_lookback_period; validate no feature value depends on data after the prediction timestamp

consequence.description: Skipping time boundary enforcement introduces look-ahead bias where models learn from future information, causing live trading performance to diverge dramatically from backtested results

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-025

core.when: When implementing train/test/trade time period splitting or validation
core.action: Assume the framework handles training/testing/trading time period boundaries correctly — the framework does not enforce temporal separation between train/test/trade phases

consequence.description: Without proper time period boundary handling, test set data can leak into training features, causing look-ahead bias that makes backtest results far more optimistic than live trading performance

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-026

core.when: When setting up backtest or live trading with train/test/trade periods
core.action: Explicitly validate that train_end < test_start and test_end < live_start, and verify no data from test or live periods appears in training feature windows

consequence.description: Without explicit temporal boundary validation, data leakage silently inflates backtest returns, causing live trading to underperform expectations significantly

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-026

core.when: When implementing RL agent training or evaluation
core.action: Assume the framework's RL reward calculation accounts for transaction costs — the framework does not model transaction costs in the RL reward function

consequence.description: Without transaction cost modeling in RL rewards, the agent learns to trade excessively since it perceives zero-cost trades as free profit opportunities, causing massive losses in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-027

core.when: When designing RL reward functions for trading strategies
core.action: Subtract transaction costs from raw portfolio returns when computing RL rewards (reward = portfolio_return - transaction_cost), incorporating bid-ask spread, commission, and slippage estimates

consequence.description: Without subtracting transaction costs from RL rewards, the agent over-trades to maximize unadjusted returns, incurring losses that erode or eliminate profits in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-027

core.when: When defining state space features for RL agents
core.action: Include features in the state space that contain future information relative to the decision timestamp — the framework does not prevent future data leakage into state features

consequence.description: Including future-leaked features in state space causes the RL agent to learn patterns dependent on information unavailable at decision time, making learned policies useless in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-028

core.when: When constructing state features for RL training
core.action: Validate each state features are available at the decision timestamp (t <= current_time), verify lookback windows are properly aligned with historical data boundaries, and exclude any feature containing future values

consequence.description: Without validating feature temporal alignment, future data leaks into training, producing RL policies that exploit look-ahead patterns and fail catastrophically in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-028

core.when: When executing data pipelines during training and inference
core.action: Reuse fitted transformers across train/test boundaries or assume pipeline fit state is properly isolated — the framework does not enforce fit/transform isolation between data splits

consequence.description: Without proper fit/transform isolation, scalers or encoders leak information across train/test splits, causing data leakage that makes model performance appear inflated during validation

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-029

core.when: When designing data pipelines for RL trading systems
core.action: Create separate pipeline instances for each data split (train pipeline fits on train data only, inference pipeline fits on data up to current time), ensuring fit statistics from test/live data never leak into training transforms

consequence.description: Without isolated pipeline instances per data split, normalization parameters or encoding statistics from test data contaminate training features, producing models that fail to generalize to truly unseen data

evidence_refs.summary: e47f1ad Derived from BD: BD-GAP-029

core.when: When constructing state features from covariates in RL or ML pipelines
core.action: Include covariate values in state features before those covariates are temporally available — the framework does not enforce time-based availability constraints on covariates

consequence.description: Using temporally unavailable covariates in state space introduces look-ahead bias, causing RL policies to appear highly profitable in backtesting but lose money in live trading where those values don't exist yet
